When [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") and the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") came to power in [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") in 1933, the situation for Jews deteriorated rapidly as a direct result of Nazi policies. Many Jews fled from Europe to [Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"), the United States, and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") as a result of racial [anti-Semitic laws](/wiki/Anti-Jewish_legislation_in_pre-war_Nazi_Germany "Anti-Jewish legislation in pre-war Nazi Germany"), economic difficulties, and the fear of an impending war. [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") started in 1939, and by 1941, Hitler occupied almost all of Europe. Following the [German invasion of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") in 1941, the [Final Solution](/wiki/Final_Solution "Final Solution")—an extensive, organized effort with an unprecedented scope intended to [annihilate](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") the Jewish people—began, and resulted in the persecution and murder of Jews in Europe and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"). In Poland, three million were murdered in [gas chambers](/wiki/Gas_chambers "Gas chambers") in all concentration camps combined, with one million at the [Auschwitz](/wiki/Auschwitz "Auschwitz") camp complex alone. The [Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") is the name given to this [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide"), in which six million Jews in total were systematically murdered.

Before and during the Holocaust, enormous numbers of Jews immigrated to Mandatory Palestine. In 1944, the [Jewish insurgency in Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Jewish_insurgency_in_Mandatory_Palestine "Jewish insurgency in Mandatory Palestine") began with the aim of gaining full independence from the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). On 14 May 1948, upon the termination of the mandate, [David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion") declared the creation of the [State of Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), a [Jewish and democratic state](/wiki/Jewish_and_democratic_state "Jewish and democratic state"). Immediately afterwards, all neighboring Arab states [invaded](/wiki/1948_Arab-Israeli_War "1948 Arab-Israeli War"), and were resisted by the newly formed [Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces"). In 1949, the war ended and Israel started building its state and absorbing waves of *[Aliyah](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah")*, granting [citizenship](/wiki/Israelis "Israelis") to Jews all over the world via the [Law of Return](/wiki/Law_of_Return "Law of Return") passed in 1950. However, both the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict") and wider [Arab–Israeli conflict](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict "Arab–Israeli conflict") continue to this day.

## Culture

Main article: [Jewish culture](/wiki/Jewish_culture "Jewish culture")

### Religion

Main article: [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism")

See also: [Jewish atheism](/wiki/Jewish_atheism "Jewish atheism") and [Jewish secularism](/wiki/Jewish_secularism "Jewish secularism")

The Jewish [people](/wiki/Jewish_peoplehood "Jewish peoplehood") and the religion of [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") are strongly interrelated. [Converts to Judaism](/wiki/Conversion_to_Judaism "Conversion to Judaism") have a status within the Jewish people equal to those born into it.[[210]](#cite_note-212) However, converts who go on to practice no Judaism are likely to be viewed with skepticism.[[211]](#cite_note-213) Mainstream Judaism does not [proselytize](/wiki/Proselytism "Proselytism"), and conversion is considered a difficult task. A significant portion of conversions are undertaken by children of mixed marriages, or would-be or current spouses of Jews.[[212]](#cite_note-214)

The [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible"), a religious interpretation of the traditions and early history of the Jews, established the first of the [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religions "Abrahamic religions"), which are now practiced by 54 percent of the world. Judaism guides its adherents in both practice and belief, and has been called not only a religion, but also a "way of life,"[[213]](#cite_note-215) which has made drawing a clear distinction between Judaism, [Jewish culture](/wiki/Jewish_culture "Jewish culture"), and [Jewish identity](/wiki/Jewish_identity "Jewish identity") rather difficult. Throughout history, in eras and places as diverse as the ancient [Hellenic](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") world,[[214]](#cite_note-216) in Europe before and after [The Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/The_Age_of_Enlightenment "The Age of Enlightenment") (see [Haskalah](/wiki/Haskalah "Haskalah")),[[215]](#cite_note-217) in [Islamic Spain and Portugal](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus"),[[216]](#cite_note-Sharot2930-218) in [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"),[[216]](#cite_note-Sharot2930-218) [India](/wiki/Indian_Jews "Indian Jews"),[[217]](#cite_note-219) [China](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_China "History of the Jews in China"),[[218]](#cite_note-220) or the contemporary [United States](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews")[[219]](#cite_note-221) and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"),[[220]](#cite_note-222) cultural phenomena have developed that are in some sense characteristically Jewish without being at all specifically religious. Some factors in this come from within Judaism, others from the interaction of Jews or specific communities of Jews with their surroundings, and still others from the inner social and cultural dynamics of the community, as opposed to from the religion itself. This phenomenon has led to considerably different [Jewish cultures](/wiki/Jewish_culture "Jewish culture") unique to their own communities.[[221]](#cite_note-223)

### Languages

Main article: [Jewish languages](/wiki/Jewish_languages "Jewish languages")

[Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") is the [liturgical language](/wiki/Liturgical_language "Liturgical language") of Judaism (termed *lashon ha-kodesh*, "the holy tongue"), the language in which most of the Hebrew scriptures ([Tanakh](/wiki/Tanakh "Tanakh")) were composed, and the daily speech of the Jewish people for centuries. By the 5th century BCE, [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic_language "Aramaic language"), a closely related tongue, joined Hebrew as the spoken language in [Judea](/wiki/Judea "Judea").[[222]](#cite_note-Grintz-224) By the 3rd century BCE, some Jews of the diaspora were speaking [Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek").[[223]](#cite_note-225) Others, such as in the Jewish communities of [Asoristan](/wiki/Asoristan "Asoristan"), known to Jews as Babylonia, were speaking Hebrew and [Aramaic](/wiki/Jewish_Babylonian_Aramaic "Jewish Babylonian Aramaic"), the languages of the [Babylonian Talmud](/wiki/Babylonian_Talmud "Babylonian Talmud"). Dialects of these same languages were also used by the Jews of [Syria Palaestina](/wiki/Syria_Palaestina "Syria Palaestina") at that time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For centuries, Jews worldwide have spoken the local or dominant languages of the regions they migrated to, often developing distinctive [dialectal](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") forms or branches that became independent languages. [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language") is the Judaeo-German language developed by [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews") who migrated to [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"). [Ladino](/wiki/Judaeo-Spanish "Judaeo-Spanish") is the Judaeo-Spanish language developed by [Sephardic](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews") Jews who migrated to the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula"). Due to many factors, including the impact of [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") on European Jewry, the [Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim countries](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_and_Muslim_countries "Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim countries"), and widespread emigration from other Jewish communities around the world, ancient and distinct [Jewish languages](/wiki/Jewish_languages "Jewish languages") of several communities, including [Judaeo-Georgian](/wiki/Judaeo-Georgian "Judaeo-Georgian"), [Judaeo-Arabic](/wiki/Judeo-Arabic_languages "Judeo-Arabic languages"), [Judaeo-Berber](/wiki/Judeo-Berber_language "Judeo-Berber language"), [Krymchak](/wiki/Krymchak_language "Krymchak language"), [Judaeo-Malayalam](/wiki/Judaeo-Malayalam "Judaeo-Malayalam") and many others, have largely fallen out of use.[[6]](#cite_note-Languages-7)

For over sixteen centuries Hebrew was used almost exclusively as a liturgical language, and as the language in which most books had been written on Judaism, with a few speaking only Hebrew on the [Sabbath](/wiki/Shabbat "Shabbat").[[224]](#cite_note-226) Hebrew was revived as a spoken language by [Eliezer ben Yehuda](/wiki/Eliezer_ben_Yehuda "Eliezer ben Yehuda"), who arrived in [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)") in 1881. It had not been used as a [mother tongue](/wiki/Mother_tongue "Mother tongue") since [Tannaic](/wiki/Tannaim "Tannaim") times.[[222]](#cite_note-Grintz-224) [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew") is designated as the "State language" of Israel.[[225]](#cite_note-227)

Despite efforts to revive Hebrew as the national language of the Jewish people, knowledge of the language is not commonly possessed by Jews worldwide and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") has emerged as the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of the Jewish diaspora.[[226]](#cite_note-228)[[227]](#cite_note-229)[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-231)[[230]](#cite_note-232) Although many Jews once had sufficient knowledge of Hebrew to study the classic literature, and [Jewish languages](/wiki/Jewish_languages "Jewish languages") like [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish") and [Ladino](/wiki/Judaeo-Spanish "Judaeo-Spanish") were commonly used as recently as the early 20th century, most Jews lack such knowledge today and English has by and large superseded most Jewish vernaculars.
The three most commonly spoken languages among Jews today are Hebrew, English, and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"). Some [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), particularly [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), are also widely used.[[6]](#cite_note-Languages-7) Yiddish has been spoken by more Jews in history than any other language,[[231]](#cite_note-233) but it is far less used today following [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") and the adoption of [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew") by the [Zionist movement](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") and the [State of Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"). In some places, the mother language of the Jewish community differs from that of the general population or the dominant group. For example, in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), the Ashkenazic majority has adopted English, while the Sephardic minority uses French as its primary language.[[232]](#cite_note-forward-234)[[233]](#cite_note-235)[[234]](#cite_note-236) Similarly, [South African Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_South_Africa "History of the Jews in South Africa") adopted English rather than [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans").[[235]](#cite_note-237) Due to both Czarist and Soviet policies,[[236]](#cite_note-238)[[237]](#cite_note-239) Russian has superseded Yiddish as the language of [Russian Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Russia "History of the Jews in Russia"), but these policies have also affected neighboring communities.[[238]](#cite_note-240) Today, Russian is the first language for many Jewish communities in a number of [Post-Soviet states](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states"), such as [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine")[[239]](#cite_note-241)[[240]](#cite_note-google-242)[[241]](#cite_note-google2-243)[[242]](#cite_note-244) and [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"),[[243]](#cite_note-jewishvirtuallibrary2-245)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] as well as for Ashkenazic Jews in [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan"),[[244]](#cite_note-Maoz-246)[[245]](#cite_note-247) Georgia,[[246]](#cite_note-248) and [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan").[[247]](#cite_note-249)[[248]](#cite_note-250) Although communities in [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") today are small and dwindling, Jews there had shifted from a multilingual group to a monolingual one (or nearly so), speaking French in [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"),[[249]](#cite_note-251) [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"),[[244]](#cite_note-Maoz-246) and the city of [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis"),[[250]](#cite_note-252)[[251]](#cite_note-jdc-253) while most North Africans continue to use [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") or Berber as their mother tongue.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Leadership

Main article: [Jewish leadership](/wiki/Jewish_leadership "Jewish leadership")

There is no single governing body for the Jewish community, nor a single authority with responsibility for religious doctrine.[[252]](#cite_note-254) Instead, a variety of secular and religious institutions at the local, national, and international levels lead various parts of the Jewish community on a variety of issues.[[253]](#cite_note-255) Today, many countries have a [Chief Rabbi](/wiki/Chief_Rabbi "Chief Rabbi") who serves as a representative of that country's Jewry. Although many [Hasidic Jews](/wiki/Hasidic_Judaism "Hasidic Judaism") follow a certain hereditary [Hasidic dynasty](/wiki/List_of_Hasidic_dynasties "List of Hasidic dynasties"), there is no one commonly accepted leader of all Hasidic Jews. Many Jews believe that the [Messiah](/wiki/Messiah_in_Judaism "Messiah in Judaism") will act a unifying leader for Jews and the entire world.[[254]](#cite_note-256)

### Theories on ancient Jewish national identity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Hebrew_Bible_MET_DP-15507-001.jpg/250px-Hebrew_Bible_MET_DP-15507-001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hebrew_Bible_MET_DP-15507-001.jpg)

Bible manuscript in Hebrew, 14th century. Hebrew language and alphabet were the cornerstones of the Jewish national identity in antiquity.

A number of modern scholars of nationalism support the existence of Jewish national identity in antiquity. One of them is David Goodblatt,[[255]](#cite_note-257) who generally believes in the existence of nationalism before the modern period. In his view, the Bible, the parabiblical literature and the Jewish national history provide the base for a Jewish collective identity. Although many of the ancient Jews were illiterate (as were their neighbors), their national narrative was reinforced through public readings. The Hebrew language also constructed and preserved national identity. Although it was not widely spoken after the 5th century BCE, Goodblatt states:[[256]](#cite_note-258)[[257]](#cite_note-259)[[258]](#cite_note-260)

> the mere presence of the language in spoken or written form could invoke the concept of a Jewish national identity. Even if one knew no Hebrew or was illiterate, one could recognize that a group of signs was in Hebrew script. ... It was the language of the Israelite ancestors, the national literature, and the national religion. As such it was inseparable from the national identity. Indeed its mere presence in visual or aural medium could invoke that identity.

[Anthony D. Smith](/wiki/Anthony_D._Smith "Anthony D. Smith"), an historical sociologist considered one of the founders of the field of [nationalism studies](/wiki/Nationalism_studies "Nationalism studies"), wrote that the Jews of the late Second Temple period provide "a closer approximation to the ideal type of the [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation") [...] than perhaps anywhere else in the ancient world." He adds that this observation "must make us wary of pronouncing too readily against the possibility of the nation, and even a form of [religious nationalism](/wiki/Religious_nationalism "Religious nationalism"), before the onset of modernity."[[140]](#cite_note-Smith-1993-142) Agreeing with Smith, Goodblatt suggests omitting the qualifier "religious" from Smith's definition of ancient Jewish nationalism, noting that, according to Smith, a religious component in national memories and culture is common even in the modern era.[[141]](#cite_note-Goodblatt-2006-143) This view is echoed by political scientist [Tom Garvin](/wiki/Tom_Garvin "Tom Garvin"), who writes that "something strangely like modern nationalism is documented for many peoples in medieval times and in classical times as well," citing the ancient Jews as one of several "obvious examples", alongside the [classical Greeks](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece") and the [Gaulish](/wiki/Gauls "Gauls") and [British Celts](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons").[[142]](#cite_note-auto-144)

[Fergus Millar](/wiki/Fergus_Millar "Fergus Millar") suggests that the sources of Jewish national identity and their early nationalist movements in the first and second centuries CE included several key elements: the Bible as both a national history and legal source, the Hebrew language as a national language, a system of law, and social institutions such as schools, synagogues, and Sabbath worship.[[259]](#cite_note-261) [Adrian Hastings](/wiki/Adrian_Hastings "Adrian Hastings") argued that Jews are the "true proto-nation", that through the model of ancient Israel found in the Hebrew Bible, provided the world with the original concept of nationhood which later influenced Christian nations. However, following [Jerusalem's destruction](/wiki/Siege_of_Jerusalem_(70_CE) "Siege of Jerusalem (70 CE)") in the first century CE, Jews ceased to be a political entity and did not resemble a traditional nation-state for almost two millennia. Despite this, they maintained their national identity through collective memory, religion and sacred texts, even without land or political power, and remained a nation rather than just an ethnic group, eventually leading to the rise of [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") and the establishment of Israel.[[143]](#cite_note-Hastings-1997-145)

[Steven Weitzman](/wiki/Steven_Weitzman_(scholar) "Steven Weitzman (scholar)") suggests that Jewish nationalist sentiment in antiquity was encouraged because under foreign rule (Persians, Greeks, Romans) Jews were able to claim that they were an ancient nation. This claim was based on the preservation and reverence of their scriptures, the Hebrew language, the Temple and priesthood, and other traditions of their ancestors.[[260]](#cite_note-262) [Doron Mendels](/wiki/Doron_Mendels "Doron Mendels") further observes that the Hasmonean kingdom, one of the few examples of indigenous statehood at its time, significantly reinforced Jewish national consciousness. The memory of this period of independence contributed to the persistent efforts to revive Jewish sovereignty in Judea, leading to the major revolts against Roman rule in the 1st and 2nd centuries CE.[[261]](#cite_note-263)

## Demographics

Further information: [Jewish population by country](/wiki/Jewish_population_by_country "Jewish population by country")

### Ethnic divisions

Main article: [Jewish ethnic divisions](/wiki/Jewish_ethnic_divisions "Jewish ethnic divisions")

Within the world's [Jewish population](/wiki/Jewish_population "Jewish population") there are distinct ethnic divisions, most of which are primarily the result of geographic branching from an originating [Israelite](/wiki/Israelite "Israelite") population, and subsequent independent evolutions. An array of Jewish communities was established by Jewish settlers in various places around the [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World"), often at great distances from one another, resulting in effective and often long-term isolation. During the [millennia](/wiki/Millennia "Millennia") of the [Jewish diaspora](/wiki/Jewish_diaspora "Jewish diaspora") the communities would develop under the influence of their local environments: [political](/wiki/Politics "Politics"), [cultural](/wiki/Culture "Culture"), [natural](/wiki/Nature "Nature"), and populational. Today, manifestations of these differences among the Jews can be observed in [Jewish cultural expressions](/wiki/Jewish_culture "Jewish culture") of each community, including [Jewish linguistic diversity](/wiki/Jewish_languages "Jewish languages"), culinary preferences, liturgical practices, religious interpretations, as well as degrees and sources of [genetic admixture](/wiki/Genetic_admixture "Genetic admixture").[[262]](#cite_note-264)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/F%C3%AAte_juive_%C3%A0_T%C3%A9touan%2C_Alfred_Dehodencq%2C_1865.jpg/250px-F%C3%AAte_juive_%C3%A0_T%C3%A9touan%2C_Alfred_Dehodencq%2C_1865.jpg)](/wiki/File:F%C3%AAte_juive_%C3%A0_T%C3%A9touan,_Alfred_Dehodencq,_1865.jpg)

Sephardic Jewish festival in [Tétouan](/wiki/T%C3%A9touan "Tétouan"), Morocco, [Alfred Dehodencq](/wiki/Alfred_Dehodencq "Alfred Dehodencq"), 1865, [Paris Museum of Jewish Art and History](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_d%27Art_et_d%27Histoire_du_Juda%C3%AFsme "Musée d'Art et d'Histoire du Judaïsme")

Jews are often identified as belonging to one of two major groups: the *[Ashkenazim](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews")* and the *[Sephardim](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews")*. Ashkenazim are so named in reference to their geographical origins (their ancestors' culture coalesced in the [Rhineland](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews#etymology "Ashkenazi Jews"), an area historically referred to by Jews as [Ashkenaz](/wiki/Ashkenaz "Ashkenaz")). Similarly, Sephardim ([Sefarad](/wiki/Sefarad "Sefarad") meaning "[Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain")" in Hebrew) are named in reference their origins in [Iberia](/wiki/Iberian_peninsula "Iberian peninsula"). The diverse groups of Jews of the Middle East and North Africa are often collectively referred to as *Sephardim* together with Sephardim proper for liturgical reasons having to do with their [prayer rites](/wiki/Nusach_(Jewish_custom) "Nusach (Jewish custom)"). A common term for many of these non-Spanish Jews who are sometimes still broadly grouped as Sephardim is *[Mizrahim](/wiki/Mizrahi_Jews "Mizrahi Jews")* (lit. 'easterners' in Hebrew). Nevertheless, Mizrahis and Sepharadim are usually ethnically distinct.[[263]](#cite_note-265)

Smaller groups include, but are not restricted to, [Indian Jews](/wiki/Jews_in_India "Jews in India") such as the [Bene Israel](/wiki/Bene_Israel "Bene Israel"), [Bnei Menashe](/wiki/Bnei_Menashe "Bnei Menashe"), [Cochin Jews](/wiki/Cochin_Jews "Cochin Jews"), and [Bene Ephraim](/wiki/Bene_Ephraim "Bene Ephraim"); the [Romaniotes](/wiki/Romaniote_Jews "Romaniote Jews") of Greece; the [Italian Jews](/wiki/Italian_rite_Jews "Italian rite Jews") ("Italkim" or "Bené Roma"); the [Teimanim](/wiki/Teimanim "Teimanim") from [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen"); various [African Jews](/wiki/Jews_and_Judaism_in_Africa "Jews and Judaism in Africa"), including most numerously the [Beta Israel](/wiki/Beta_Israel "Beta Israel") of [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"); and [Chinese Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_China "History of the Jews in China"), most notably the [Kaifeng Jews](/wiki/Kaifeng_Jews "Kaifeng Jews"), as well as various other distinct but now almost extinct communities.[[264]](#cite_note-EJ571-266)

The divisions between all these groups are approximate and their boundaries are not always clear. The Mizrahim for example, are a heterogeneous collection of [North African](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), [Central Asian](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), [Caucasian](/wiki/Caucasus_(geographic_region) "Caucasus (geographic region)"), and Middle Eastern Jewish communities that are no closer related to each other than they are to any of the earlier mentioned Jewish groups. In modern usage, however, the Mizrahim are sometimes termed *Sephardi* due to similar styles of liturgy, despite independent development from Sephardim proper. Thus, among Mizrahim there are [Egyptian Jews](/wiki/Egyptian_Jews "Egyptian Jews"), [Iraqi Jews](/wiki/Iraqi_Jews "Iraqi Jews"), [Lebanese Jews](/wiki/Lebanese_Jews "Lebanese Jews"), [Kurdish Jews](/wiki/Kurdish_Jews "Kurdish Jews"), [Moroccan Jews](/wiki/Moroccan_Jews "Moroccan Jews"), [Libyan Jews](/wiki/Libyan_Jews "Libyan Jews"), [Syrian Jews](/wiki/Syrian_Jews "Syrian Jews"), [Bukharian Jews](/wiki/Bukharian_Jews "Bukharian Jews"), [Mountain Jews](/wiki/Mountain_Jews "Mountain Jews"), [Georgian Jews](/wiki/Georgian_Jews "Georgian Jews"), [Iranian Jews](/wiki/Iranian_Jews "Iranian Jews"), [Afghan Jews](/wiki/Afghan_Jews "Afghan Jews"), and various others. The [Teimanim](/wiki/Teimanim "Teimanim") from [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen") are sometimes included, although their style of liturgy is unique and they differ in respect to the admixture found among them to that found in Mizrahim. In addition, there is a differentiation made between Sephardi migrants who established themselves in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") after the expulsion of the Jews from Spain and Portugal in the 1490s and the pre-existing Jewish communities in those regions.[[264]](#cite_note-EJ571-266)

Ashkenazi Jews represent the bulk of modern Jewry, with at least 70 percent of Jews worldwide (and up to 90 percent prior to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust")). As a result of their [emigration](/wiki/Emigration "Emigration") from [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), Ashkenazim also represent the overwhelming majority of Jews in the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") continents, in countries such as the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). In [France](/wiki/France "France"), the immigration of Jews from [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria") (Sephardim) has led them to outnumber the Ashkenazim.[[264]](#cite_note-EJ571-266) Only in [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") is the Jewish population representative of all groups, a [melting pot](/wiki/Melting_pot "Melting pot") independent of each group's proportion within the overall world Jewish population.[[265]](#cite_note-267)

### Genetic studies

Main article: [Genetic studies on Jews](/wiki/Genetic_studies_on_Jews "Genetic studies on Jews")

[Y DNA](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome") studies tend to imply a small number of founders in an old population whose members parted and followed different migration paths.[[266]](#cite_note-hammer2000-268) In most Jewish populations, these male line ancestors appear to have been mainly [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"). For example, [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews") share more common paternal lineages with other Jewish and Middle Eastern groups than with non-Jewish populations in areas where Jews lived in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), and the French [Rhine Valley](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"). This is consistent with Jewish traditions in placing most Jewish paternal origins in the region of the Middle East.[[267]](#cite_note-Nebel_2001-269)[[268]](#cite_note-270)

Conversely, the maternal lineages of Jewish populations, studied by looking at [mitochondrial DNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA"), are generally more heterogeneous.[[269]](#cite_note-Behar2008b-271) Scholars such as [Harry Ostrer](/wiki/Harry_Ostrer "Harry Ostrer") and Raphael Falk believe this indicates that many Jewish males found new mates from European and other communities in the places where they migrated in the diaspora after fleeing ancient Israel.[[270]](#cite_note-Lewontin-272) In contrast, Behar has found evidence that about 40 percent of Ashkenazi Jews originate maternally from just four female founders, who were of Middle Eastern origin. The populations of Sephardi and Mizrahi Jewish communities "showed no evidence for a narrow founder effect."[[269]](#cite_note-Behar2008b-271) Subsequent studies carried out by Feder et al. confirmed the large portion of non-local maternal origin among Ashkenazi Jews. Reflecting on their findings related to the maternal origin of Ashkenazi Jews, the authors conclude "Clearly, the differences between Jews and non-Jews are far larger than those observed among the Jewish communities. Hence, differences between the Jewish communities can be overlooked when non-Jews are included in the comparisons."[[14]](#cite_note-Abraham_2010-15)[[271]](#cite_note-273)[[272]](#cite_note-Ostrer-2013-274) However, a 2025 genetic study on the Ashkenazi Jewish founder population supports the presence of a substantial Near Eastern component in the maternal lineages. Analyses of mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) indicate that the core founder lineages, estimated at around 54, likely originated from the Near East, with these founder signatures appearing in multiple copies across the population. While later admixture introduced additional mtDNA lineages, these absorbed lineages are distinguishable from the original founders. The findings are consistent with genome-wide Identity-by-Descent and Lineage Extinction analyses, reinforcing the Near Eastern origin of the Ashkenazi maternal founders.[[273]](#cite_note-Livni-2025-275) A study showed that 7% of Ashkenazi Jews have the haplogroup G2c, which is mainly found in [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") and on lower scales all major Jewish groups, Palestinians, Syrians, and Lebanese.[[274]](#cite_note-276)[[275]](#cite_note-277)

Studies of [autosomal DNA](/wiki/Autosome "Autosome"), which look at the entire DNA mixture, have become increasingly important as the technology develops. They show that Jewish populations have tended to form relatively closely related groups in independent communities, with most in a community sharing significant ancestry in common.[[276]](#cite_note-278) For Jewish populations of the diaspora, the genetic composition of [Ashkenazi](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews"), [Sephardic](/wiki/Sephardic_Jews "Sephardic Jews"), and [Mizrahi](/wiki/Mizrahi_Jews "Mizrahi Jews") Jewish populations show a predominant amount of shared Middle Eastern ancestry. According to Behar, the most parsimonious explanation for this shared Middle Eastern ancestry is that it is "consistent with the historical formulation of the Jewish people as descending from ancient [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrews "Hebrews") and [Israelite](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites") residents of the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant")" and "the dispersion of the people of ancient Israel throughout the [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World")".[[277]](#cite_note-discovermagazine-279) [North African](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula") and others of [Iberian](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") origin show variable frequencies of admixture with non-Jewish historical host populations among the maternal lines. In the case of Ashkenazi and Sephardi Jews (in particular [Moroccan Jews](/wiki/Moroccan_Jews "Moroccan Jews")), who are closely related, the source of non-Jewish admixture is mainly [Southern European](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"), while Mizrahi Jews show evidence of admixture with other Middle Eastern populations. Behar *et al.* have remarked on a close relationship between Ashkenazi Jews and modern [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians").[[277]](#cite_note-discovermagazine-279)[[278]](#cite_note-zooss-280) A 2001 study found that Jews were more closely related to groups of the Fertile Crescent (Kurds, Turks, and Armenians) than to their Arab neighbors, whose genetic signature was found in geographic patterns reflective of Islamic conquests.[[267]](#cite_note-Nebel_2001-269)[[279]](#cite_note-281)

The studies also show that [Sephardic Bnei Anusim](/wiki/Sephardic_Bnei_Anusim "Sephardic Bnei Anusim") (descendants of the "[anusim](/wiki/Anusim "Anusim")" who were [forced to convert](/wiki/Forced_conversion "Forced conversion") to [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism")), which comprise up to 19.8 percent of the population of today's [Iberia](/wiki/Iberia "Iberia") ([Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal")) and at least 10 percent of the population of [Ibero-America](/wiki/Ibero-America "Ibero-America") ([Hispanic America](/wiki/Hispanic_America "Hispanic America") and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")), have Sephardic Jewish ancestry within the last few centuries. The [Bene Israel](/wiki/Bene_Israel "Bene Israel") and [Cochin Jews](/wiki/Cochin_Jews "Cochin Jews") of [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Beta Israel](/wiki/Beta_Israel "Beta Israel") of [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), and a portion of the [Lemba people](/wiki/Lemba_people "Lemba people") of [Southern Africa](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa"), despite more closely resembling the local populations of their native countries, have also been thought to have some more remote ancient Jewish ancestry.[[280]](#cite_note-282)[[277]](#cite_note-discovermagazine-279)[[281]](#cite_note-in.reuters.com-283)[[272]](#cite_note-Ostrer-2013-274) Views on the Lemba have changed and genetic [Y-DNA](/wiki/Y-DNA "Y-DNA") analyses in the 2000s have established a partially Middle-Eastern origin for a portion of the male Lemba population but have been unable to narrow this down further.[[282]](#cite_note-SpurdleJenkins-284)[[283]](#cite_note-Soodyall-285)

### Population centers

For a more comprehensive list, see [Jewish population by city](/wiki/Jewish_population_by_city "Jewish population by city").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Purim_2012_Williamsburg_Brooklyn_01.jpg/250px-Purim_2012_Williamsburg_Brooklyn_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Purim_2012_Williamsburg_Brooklyn_01.jpg)

[New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") is home to 960,000 Jews, making it the [largest Jewish community](/wiki/Jews_in_New_York_City "Jews in New York City") outside of Israel.

Although historically, Jews have been found all over the world, in the decades since World War II and the establishment of Israel, they have increasingly concentrated in a small number of countries.[[284]](#cite_note-286)[[285]](#cite_note-287) In 2021, [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") together accounted for over 85 percent of the global Jewish population, with approximately 45.3% and 39.6% of the world's Jews, respectively.[[2]](#cite_note-JDB-3) More than half (51.2%) of world Jewry resides in just ten metropolitan areas. As of 2021, these ten areas were [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Gush_Dan "Gush Dan"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"), [Jerusalem](/wiki/Greater_Jerusalem "Greater Jerusalem"), [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa_metropolitan_area "Haifa metropolitan area"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles "Greater Los Angeles"), [Miami](/wiki/Miami_metropolitan_area "Miami metropolitan area"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris_metropolitan_area "Paris metropolitan area"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"), and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area "Chicago metropolitan area"). The Tel Aviv metro area has the highest percent of Jews among the total population (94.8%), followed by Jerusalem (72.3%), Haifa (73.1%), and Beersheba (60.4%), the balance mostly being Israeli Arabs. Outside Israel, the highest percent of Jews in a metropolitan area was in New York (10.8%), followed by Miami (8.7%), Philadelphia (6.8%), San Francisco (5.1%), Washington (4.7%), Los Angeles (4.7%), Toronto (4.5%), and Baltimore (4.1%).[[2]](#cite_note-JDB-3)

As of 2010, there were nearly 14 million Jews around the world, roughly 0.2% of the world's population at the time.[[286]](#cite_note-Pew-2015-288) According to the 2007 estimates of [The Jewish People Policy Planning Institute](/wiki/The_Jewish_People_Policy_Planning_Institute "The Jewish People Policy Planning Institute"), the world's Jewish population is 13.2 million.[[287]](#cite_note-haaretz.com-289) This statistic incorporates both practicing Jews affiliated with [synagogues](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") and the Jewish community, and approximately 4.5 million unaffiliated and [secular Jews](/wiki/Jewish_secularism "Jewish secularism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to [Sergio Della Pergola](/wiki/Sergio_Della_Pergola "Sergio Della Pergola"), a demographer of the [Jewish population](/wiki/Jewish_population "Jewish population"), in 2021 there were about 6.8 million Jews in Israel, 6 million in the United States, and 2.3 million in the rest of the world.[[2]](#cite_note-JDB-3)

#### Israel

Main article: [Israeli Jews](/wiki/Israeli_Jews "Israeli Jews")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Mahane_Yehuda_Market_P1020256.JPG/250px-Mahane_Yehuda_Market_P1020256.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mahane_Yehuda_Market_P1020256.JPG)

Jewish people in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), Israel

[Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), the Jewish nation-state, is the only country in which Jews make up a majority of the citizens.[[288]](#cite_note-290) Israel was established as an independent [democratic](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") and Jewish state on 14 May 1948.[[289]](#cite_note-cia-291) Of the 120 members in its parliament, the [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset"),[[290]](#cite_note-292) as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Jews&action=edit), 14 members of the Knesset are [Arab citizens of Israel](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel") (not including the Druze), most representing Arab political parties. One of Israel's [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Israel "Supreme Court of Israel") judges is also an Arab citizen of Israel.[[291]](#cite_note-293)

Between 1948 and 1958, the Jewish population rose from 800,000 to two million.[[292]](#cite_note-294) Currently, Jews account for 75.4 percent of the Israeli population, or 6 million people.[[293]](#cite_note-295)[[294]](#cite_note-296) The early years of the State of Israel were marked by the [mass immigration](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah") of [Holocaust survivors](/wiki/Holocaust_survivors "Holocaust survivors") in the [aftermath of the Holocaust](/wiki/Aftermath_of_the_Holocaust "Aftermath of the Holocaust") and Jews [fleeing Arab lands](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_and_Muslim_countries "Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim countries").[[295]](#cite_note-persecution-297) Israel also has a large population of [Ethiopian Jews](/wiki/Ethiopian_Jews "Ethiopian Jews"), many of whom were airlifted to Israel in the late 1980s and early 1990s.[[296]](#cite_note-298)[[297]](#cite_note-299) Between 1974 and 1979 nearly 227,258 immigrants arrived in Israel, about half being from the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[298]](#cite_note-300) This period also saw an increase in [immigration to Israel](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah") from [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), and [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America").[[299]](#cite_note-301)

A trickle of immigrants from other communities has also arrived, including [Indian Jews](/wiki/Indian_Jews "Indian Jews") and others, as well as some descendants of [Ashkenazi](/wiki/Ashkenazi "Ashkenazi") Holocaust survivors who had settled in countries such as the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"). Some Jews have emigrated from Israel elsewhere, because of economic problems or disillusionment with political conditions and the continuing [Arab–Israeli conflict](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict "Arab–Israeli conflict"). Jewish Israeli emigrants are known as [yordim](/wiki/Yerida "Yerida").[[300]](#cite_note-Dosick_2007,_p._340-302)

#### Diaspora (outside Israel)

Main article: [Jewish diaspora](/wiki/Jewish_diaspora "Jewish diaspora")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Happynewyearcard.jpg/250px-Happynewyearcard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Happynewyearcard.jpg)

In this [Rosh Hashana](/wiki/Rosh_Hashana "Rosh Hashana") greeting card from the early 1900s, Russian Jews, packs in hand, gaze at the American relatives beckoning them to the United States. Over two million Jews fled the [pogroms](/wiki/Pogroms "Pogroms") of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") to the safety of the U.S. between 1881 and 1924.[[301]](#cite_note-303)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Birobidjan_mainsquare.jpg/250px-Birobidjan_mainsquare.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birobidjan_mainsquare.jpg)

A [menorah](/wiki/Menorah_(Temple) "Menorah (Temple)") dominating the main square in [Birobidzhan](/wiki/Birobidzhan "Birobidzhan"). An estimated 70,000 [Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Russia_and_the_Soviet_Union "History of the Jews in Russia and the Soviet Union") live in [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia").[[302]](#cite_note-304)

The waves of [immigration to the United States](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") and elsewhere at the turn of the 19th century, the founding of [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") and later events, including [pogroms](/wiki/Pogroms "Pogroms") in Imperial Russia (mostly within the [Pale of Settlement](/wiki/Pale_of_Settlement "Pale of Settlement") in present-day Ukraine, Moldova, Belarus and eastern Poland), the massacre of European Jewry during [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"), and the founding of the [state of Israel](/wiki/State_of_Israel "State of Israel"), with the subsequent [Jewish exodus from Arab lands](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_lands "Jewish exodus from Arab lands"), all resulted in substantial shifts in the population centers of world Jewry by the end of the 20th century.[[303]](#cite_note-305)

More than half of the Jews live in the Diaspora (see Population table). Currently, the largest Jewish community outside Israel, and either the largest or second-largest Jewish community in the world, is located in the United States, with 6 million to 7.5 million Jews by various estimates. Elsewhere in the Americas, there are also large Jewish populations in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") (315,000), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") (180,000–300,000), and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") (196,000–600,000), and smaller populations in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay"), [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") and several other countries (see [History of the Jews in Latin America](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Latin_America "History of the Jews in Latin America")).[[304]](#cite_note-JPPI2007-306) According to a 2010 [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") study, about 470,000 people of Jewish heritage live in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean").[[286]](#cite_note-Pew-2015-288) Demographers disagree on whether the United States has a larger Jewish population than Israel, with many maintaining that Israel surpassed the United States in Jewish population during the 2000s, while others maintain that the United States still has the largest Jewish population in the world. Currently, a major national Jewish population survey is planned to ascertain whether or not Israel has overtaken the United States in Jewish population.[[305]](#cite_note-307)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/DAVID_BEN_GURION_WITH_MEMBERS_OF_JEWISH_ZIONIST_YOUTH_MOVEMENT_IN_TALLIN_IN_ESTONIA._%D7%93%D7%95%D7%93_%D7%91%D7%9F_%D7%92%D7%95%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%95%D7%9F_%D7%95%D7%97%D7%91%D7%A8%D7%99_%D7%AA%D7%A0%D7%95%D7%A2%D7%AA_%D7%94%D7%A0%D7%95%D7%A2%D7%A8_%D7%94%D7%A6%D7%99%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%99%D7%AA%2C_%D7%91%D7%98%D7%90%D7%9C%D7%99%D7%9F%2C_%D7%90%D7%A1%D7%98%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%99%D7%94.D683-119.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:DAVID_BEN_GURION_WITH_MEMBERS_OF_JEWISH_ZIONIST_YOUTH_MOVEMENT_IN_TALLIN_IN_ESTONIA._%D7%93%D7%95%D7%93_%D7%91%D7%9F_%D7%92%D7%95%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%95%D7%9F_%D7%95%D7%97%D7%91%D7%A8%D7%99_%D7%AA%D7%A0%D7%95%D7%A2%D7%AA_%D7%94%D7%A0%D7%95%D7%A2%D7%A8_%D7%94%D7%A6%D7%99%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%99%D7%AA,_%D7%91%D7%98%D7%90%D7%9C%D7%99%D7%9F,_%D7%90%D7%A1%D7%98%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%99%D7%94.D683-119.jpg)

The Jewish [Zionist](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") Youth Movement in [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn"), Estonia, on 1 September 1933

[Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe")'s largest Jewish community, and the third-largest Jewish community in the world, can be found in [France](/wiki/France "France"), home to between 483,000 and 500,000 Jews, the majority of whom are immigrants or refugees from North African countries such as [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"), [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), and [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia") (or their descendants).[[306]](#cite_note-308) The [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") has a Jewish community of 292,000. In [Eastern Europe](/wiki/East_Europe "East Europe"), the exact figures are difficult to establish. The number of Jews in Russia varies widely according to whether a source uses census data (which requires a person to choose a single nationality among choices that include "Russian" and "Jewish") or eligibility for immigration to Israel (which requires that a person have one or more Jewish grandparents). According to the latter criteria, the heads of the Russian Jewish community assert that up to 1.5 million Russians are eligible for [aliyah](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah").[[307]](#cite_note-309)[[308]](#cite_note-310) In [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), the 102,000 Jews registered with the Jewish community are a slowly declining population,[[309]](#cite_note-311) despite the immigration of tens of thousands of Jews from the former [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") since the fall of the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Berlin_Wall "Berlin Wall").[[310]](#cite_note-312) Thousands of [Israelis](/wiki/Israelis "Israelis") also live in Germany, either permanently or temporarily, for economic reasons.[[311]](#cite_note-313)

Prior to 1948, approximately 800,000 Jews were living in lands which now make up the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") (excluding Israel). Of these, just under two-thirds lived in the French-controlled [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb") region, 15 to 20 percent in the [Kingdom of Iraq](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iraq "Kingdom of Iraq"), approximately 10 percent in the [Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt") and approximately 7 percent in the [Kingdom of Yemen](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yemen "Kingdom of Yemen"). A further 200,000 lived in [Pahlavi Iran](/wiki/Pahlavi_Iran "Pahlavi Iran") and the [Republic of Turkey](/wiki/Republic_of_Turkey "Republic of Turkey"). Today, around 26,000 Jews live in Muslim-majority countries,[[312]](#cite_note-314) mainly in [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") (14,200)[[313]](#cite_note-315) and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") (9,100), while [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") (2,000), [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia") (1,000), and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") (500) host the largest communities in the Arab world.[[314]](#cite_note-316) A small-scale exodus had begun in many countries in the early decades of the 20th century, although the only substantial [aliyah](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah") came from [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen") and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria").[[315]](#cite_note-317) The [exodus from Arab and Muslim countries](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_and_Muslim_countries "Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim countries") took place primarily from 1948. The first large-scale exoduses took place in the late 1940s and early 1950s, primarily in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), Yemen and [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya"), with up to 90 percent of these communities leaving within a few years. The peak of the exodus from [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") occurred in 1956. The exodus in the Maghreb countries peaked in the 1960s. [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") was the only Arab country to see a temporary increase in its Jewish population during this period, due to an influx of refugees from other Arab countries, although by the mid-1970s the Jewish community of Lebanon had also dwindled. In the aftermath of the exodus wave from Arab states, an additional migration of [Iranian Jews](/wiki/Iranian_Jews "Iranian Jews") peaked in the 1980s when around 80 percent of Iranian Jews left the country.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Outside [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), and the rest of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), there are significant Jewish populations in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") (112,500) and [South Africa](/wiki/Jewish_population_of_South_Africa "Jewish population of South Africa") (70,000).[[47]](#cite_note-JVIL2010-48) There is also a 6,800-strong community in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").[[316]](#cite_note-318)

### Demographic changes

Main article: [Historical Jewish population comparisons](/wiki/Historical_Jewish_population_comparisons "Historical Jewish population comparisons")

#### Assimilation

Main articles: [Jewish assimilation](/wiki/Jewish_assimilation "Jewish assimilation") and [Interfaith marriage in Judaism](/wiki/Interfaith_marriage_in_Judaism "Interfaith marriage in Judaism")

Since at least the time of the [Ancient Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), a proportion of Jews have assimilated into the wider non-Jewish society around them, by either choice or force, ceasing to practice Judaism and losing their [Jewish identity](/wiki/Jewish_identity "Jewish identity").[[317]](#cite_note-Johnson171-319) Assimilation took place in all areas, and during all time periods,[[317]](#cite_note-Johnson171-319) with some Jewish communities, for example the [Kaifeng Jews](/wiki/Kaifeng_Jews "Kaifeng Jews") of [China](/wiki/China "China"), disappearing entirely.[[318]](#cite_note-320) The advent of the Jewish Enlightenment of the 18th century (see [Haskalah](/wiki/Haskalah "Haskalah")) and the subsequent [emancipation of the Jewish populations](/wiki/Jewish_emancipation "Jewish emancipation") of Europe and America in the 19th century, accelerated the situation, encouraging Jews to increasingly participate in, and become part of, [secular society](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism"). The result has been a growing trend of assimilation, as Jews marry non-Jewish spouses and stop participating in the Jewish community.[[319]](#cite_note-321)

Rates of [interreligious marriage](/wiki/Interfaith_marriage "Interfaith marriage") vary widely: In the United States, it is just under 50 percent;[[320]](#cite_note-322) in the United Kingdom, around 53 percent; in France, around 30 percent;[[321]](#cite_note-323) and in Australia and Mexico, as low as 10 percent.[[322]](#cite_note-324) In the United States, only about a third of children from intermarriages affiliate with Jewish religious practice.[[323]](#cite_note-325) The result is that most countries in the [Diaspora](/wiki/Jewish_diaspora "Jewish diaspora") have steady or slightly declining religiously Jewish populations as Jews continue to assimilate into the countries in which they live.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### War and persecution

Further information: [Persecution of Jews](/wiki/Persecution_of_Jews "Persecution of Jews"), [Antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism"), and [Jewish military history](/wiki/Jewish_military_history "Jewish military history")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/The_Emperor_sends_Vespasian_with_an_army_to_destroy_the_Jews_%28f._177v%29_Cropped.jpg/250px-The_Emperor_sends_Vespasian_with_an_army_to_destroy_the_Jews_%28f._177v%29_Cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Emperor_sends_Vespasian_with_an_army_to_destroy_the_Jews_(f._177v)_Cropped.jpg)

The Roman Emperor [Nero](/wiki/Nero "Nero") sends [Vespasian](/wiki/Vespasian "Vespasian") with an army to destroy the Jews, 69 CE.

The Jewish people and [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") have experienced various [persecutions](/wiki/Persecution "Persecution") throughout [their history](/wiki/Jewish_history "Jewish history"). During [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity") and the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") (in its later phases known as the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire")) repeatedly repressed the [Jewish population](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_the_Roman_Empire "History of the Jews in the Roman Empire"), first by ejecting them from their homelands during the pagan [Roman era](/wiki/Roman_era "Roman era") and later by officially establishing them as [second-class citizens](/wiki/Justinian_I#Suppression_of_religions "Justinian I") during the Christian Roman era.[[324]](#cite_note-326)[[325]](#cite_note-327)

According to [James Carroll](/wiki/James_P._Carroll "James P. Carroll"), "Jews accounted for 10% of the total population of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). By that ratio, if other factors had not intervened, there would be 200 million Jews in the world today, instead of something like 13 million."[[326]](#cite_note-328)

Later in [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") Western Europe, further persecutions of Jews by Christians occurred, notably during the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades")—when Jews all over Germany [were massacred](/wiki/Rhineland_massacres "Rhineland massacres")—and in a series of expulsions from the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Edict_of_Expulsion "Edict of Expulsion"), Germany, and France. Then there occurred the [largest expulsion of all](/wiki/Alhambra_Decree "Alhambra Decree"), when Spain and Portugal, after the [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista") (the Catholic Reconquest of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula")), expelled both unbaptized Sephardic Jews and the ruling Muslim [Moors](/wiki/Moors "Moors").[[327]](#cite_note-Johnson207-208-329)[[328]](#cite_note-Johnson213plus-330)

In the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States"), which existed until 1870, Jews were required to live only in specified neighborhoods called [ghettos](/wiki/Ghetto "Ghetto").[[329]](#cite_note-331)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/The_Jews_the_world_over_love_liberty_poster.jpg/250px-The_Jews_the_world_over_love_liberty_poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Jews_the_world_over_love_liberty_poster.jpg)

[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") poster showing a soldier cutting the bonds from a Jewish man, who says, "You have cut my bonds and set me free—now let me help you set others free!"

[Islam and Judaism](/wiki/Islam_and_Judaism "Islam and Judaism") have a complex relationship. Traditionally Jews and Christians living in Muslim lands, known as [dhimmis](/wiki/Dhimmis "Dhimmis"), were allowed to practice their religions and administer their internal affairs, but they were subject to certain conditions.[[330]](#cite_note-Bernard1020-332) They had to pay the [jizya](/wiki/Jizya "Jizya") (a per capita tax imposed on free adult non-Muslim males) to the Islamic state.[[330]](#cite_note-Bernard1020-332) Dhimmis had an inferior status under Islamic rule. They had several social and legal [disabilities](/wiki/Disabilities_(Jewish) "Disabilities (Jewish)") such as prohibitions against bearing arms or giving testimony in courts in cases involving Muslims.[[331]](#cite_note-333) Many of the disabilities were highly symbolic. The one described by [Bernard Lewis](/wiki/Bernard_Lewis "Bernard Lewis") as "most degrading"[[332]](#cite_note-Lewis131-334) was the requirement of [distinctive clothing](/wiki/Yellow_badge "Yellow badge"), not found in the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") or [hadith](/wiki/Hadith "Hadith") but invented in [early medieval](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages") Baghdad; its enforcement was highly erratic.[[332]](#cite_note-Lewis131-334) On the other hand, Jews rarely faced martyrdom or exile, or forced compulsion to change their religion, and they were mostly free in their choice of residence and profession.[[333]](#cite_note-335)

Notable exceptions include the massacre of Jews and forcible conversion of some Jews by the rulers of the [Almohad](/wiki/Almohad_Caliphate "Almohad Caliphate") dynasty in [Al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") in the 12th century,[[334]](#cite_note-336) as well as in [Islamic Persia](/wiki/Islamic_conquest_of_Persia "Islamic conquest of Persia"),[[335]](#cite_note-337) and the forced confinement of Moroccan Jews to walled quarters known as [mellahs](/wiki/Mellah "Mellah") beginning from the 15th century and especially in the early 19th century.[[336]](#cite_note-338) In modern times, it has become commonplace for standard [antisemitic themes to be conflated with anti-Zionist](/wiki/Anti-Zionism_and_antisemitism "Anti-Zionism and antisemitism") publications and pronouncements of Islamic movements such as [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah") and [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas"), in the pronouncements of various agencies of the [Islamic Republic of Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), and even in the newspapers and other publications of Turkish [Refah Partisi](/wiki/Refah_Partisi "Refah Partisi")."[[337]](#cite_note-Lewis_MEQ-339)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Throughout history, many rulers, empires and nations have oppressed their Jewish populations or sought to eliminate them entirely. Methods employed ranged from [expulsion](/wiki/Deportation "Deportation") to outright [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide"); within nations, often the threat of these extreme methods was sufficient to silence dissent. The [history of antisemitism](/wiki/History_of_antisemitism "History of antisemitism") includes the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade") which resulted in the massacre of Jews;[[327]](#cite_note-Johnson207-208-329) the [Spanish Inquisition](/wiki/Spanish_Inquisition "Spanish Inquisition") (led by [Tomás de Torquemada](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_de_Torquemada "Tomás de Torquemada")) and the [Portuguese Inquisition](/wiki/Portuguese_Inquisition "Portuguese Inquisition"), with their persecution and *[autos-da-fé](/wiki/Auto-da-f%C3%A9 "Auto-da-fé")* against the [New Christians](/wiki/New_Christians "New Christians") and [Marrano](/wiki/Marrano "Marrano") Jews;[[338]](#cite_note-340) the [Bohdan Chmielnicki](/wiki/Bohdan_Chmielnicki "Bohdan Chmielnicki") Cossack massacres in [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine");[[339]](#cite_note-341) the [Pogroms](/wiki/Pogrom "Pogrom") backed by the Russian [Tsars](/wiki/List_of_Russian_rulers "List of Russian rulers");[[340]](#cite_note-Johnson_1987,_pp._364–365-342) as well as expulsions from Spain, Portugal, England, France, Germany, and other countries in which the Jews had settled.[[328]](#cite_note-Johnson213plus-330) According to a 2008 study published in the *[American Journal of Human Genetics](/wiki/American_Journal_of_Human_Genetics "American Journal of Human Genetics")*, 19.8 percent of the modern [Iberian](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") population has Sephardic Jewish ancestry,[[341]](#cite_note-Adams2008-343) indicating that the number of [conversos](/wiki/Converso "Converso") may have been much higher than originally thought.[[342]](#cite_note-344)[[343]](#cite_note-345)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Bundesarchiv_N_1576_Bild-006%2C_Minsk%2C_Juden.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_N_1576_Bild-006%2C_Minsk%2C_Juden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_N_1576_Bild-006,_Minsk,_Juden.jpg)

Jews in [Minsk](/wiki/Minsk "Minsk"), 1941. Before World War II, some 40 percent of the population was Jewish. By the time the Red Army retook the city on 3 July 1944, there were only a few Jewish survivors.

The persecution reached a peak in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany")'s [Final Solution](/wiki/Final_Solution "Final Solution"), which led to [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") and the slaughter of approximately 6 million Jews.[[344]](#cite_note-346) Of the world's 16 million Jews in 1939, almost 40% were murdered in the Holocaust.[[345]](#cite_note-347) The Holocaust—the state-led systematic [persecution](/wiki/Persecution "Persecution") and [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") of European Jews (and certain communities of North African Jews in [European controlled North Africa](/wiki/History_of_North_Africa#European_colonization "History of North Africa")) and other [minority groups](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group") of Europe during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") by Germany and its [collaborators](/wiki/Collaboration_with_Nazi_Germany_and_Fascist_Italy "Collaboration with Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy")—remains the most notable modern-day persecution of Jews.[[346]](#cite_note-348) The persecution and [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") were accomplished in stages. [Legislation to remove the Jews from civil society](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws") was enacted years before the outbreak of World War II.[[347]](#cite_note-349) [Concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps") were established in which inmates were used as [slave labour](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") until they died of exhaustion or disease.[[348]](#cite_note-350) Where the [Third Reich](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") conquered new territory in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), specialized units called [Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen") murdered Jews and political opponents in mass shootings.[[349]](#cite_note-BBC-Grave-351) Jews and [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") were crammed into [ghettos](/wiki/Ghettos_in_Nazi-occupied_Europe "Ghettos in Nazi-occupied Europe") before being transported hundreds of kilometres by freight train to [extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp") where, if they survived the journey, the majority of them were murdered in gas chambers.[[350]](#cite_note-352) Virtually every arm of Germany's bureaucracy was involved in the logistics of the mass murder, turning the country into what one Holocaust scholar has called "a genocidal nation."[[351]](#cite_note-Berenbaum103-353)

#### Migrations

Further information: [Expulsions of Jews](/wiki/Expulsions_of_Jews "Expulsions of Jews")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Expulsion_judios-en.svg/250px-Expulsion_judios-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Expulsion_judios-en.svg)

Expulsions of Jews in Europe from 1100 to 1600

Throughout Jewish history, Jews have repeatedly been directly or indirectly expelled from both their original homeland, the [Land of Israel](/wiki/Land_of_Israel "Land of Israel"), and many of the areas in which they have settled. This experience as [refugees](/wiki/Jewish_refugees "Jewish refugees") has shaped [Jewish identity](/wiki/Jewish_identity "Jewish identity") and religious practice in many ways, and is thus a major element of Jewish history.[[352]](#cite_note-354) In summary, the [pogroms](/wiki/Pogrom "Pogrom") in Eastern Europe,[[340]](#cite_note-Johnson_1987,_pp._364–365-342) the rise of modern [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism"),[[353]](#cite_note-Gartner_2001,_pp._213–5-355) the Holocaust,[[354]](#cite_note-356) as well as the rise of [Arab nationalism](/wiki/Arab_nationalism "Arab nationalism"),[[355]](#cite_note-357) all served to fuel the movements and migrations of huge segments of Jewry from land to land and continent to continent until they arrived back in large numbers at their original historical homeland in Israel.[[356]](#cite_note-Gartner431-358)

In the Bible, the patriarch [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham") is described as a migrant to the land of [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan") from [Ur of the Chaldees](/wiki/Ur_of_the_Chaldees "Ur of the Chaldees").[[357]](#cite_note-359) His descendants, the [Children of Israel](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites"), undertook [the Exodus](/wiki/The_Exodus "The Exodus") (meaning "departure" or "exit" in Greek) from [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), as described in the [Book of Exodus](/wiki/Book_of_Exodus "Book of Exodus").[[358]](#cite_note-360)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Judaean_people_are_being_deported_into_exile_after_the_capture_of_Lachish.jpg/250px-Judaean_people_are_being_deported_into_exile_after_the_capture_of_Lachish.jpg)](/wiki/File:Judaean_people_are_being_deported_into_exile_after_the_capture_of_Lachish.jpg)

Residents of [Lachish](/wiki/Tel_Lachish "Tel Lachish"), [Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah"), being deported into exile following the [conquest of the city](/wiki/Siege_of_Lachish "Siege of Lachish") by the [Assyrians](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian_Empire "Neo-Assyrian Empire"), c. 701 BCE

The first movement documented in the historical record occurred with the [resettlement policy of the Neo-Assyrian Empire](/wiki/Resettlement_policy_of_the_Neo-Assyrian_Empire "Resettlement policy of the Neo-Assyrian Empire"), which mandated the deportation of conquered peoples, and it is estimated some 4,500,000 among its captive populations suffered this dislocation over three centuries of Assyrian rule.[[359]](#cite_note-Smith-Christopher-361) With regard to Israel, [Tiglath-Pileser III](/wiki/Tiglath-Pileser_III "Tiglath-Pileser III") claims he deported 80% of the population of [Lower Galilee](/wiki/Lower_Galilee "Lower Galilee"), some 13,520 people.[[360]](#cite_note-362) Some 27,000 Israelites, 20 to 25% of the population of the [Kingdom of Israel](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Israel_(Samaria) "Kingdom of Israel (Samaria)"), were described as being deported by [Sargon II](/wiki/Sargon_II "Sargon II"), and were replaced by other deported populations and sent into permanent exile by Assyria, initially to the Upper Mesopotamian provinces of the Assyrian Empire.[[361]](#cite_note-363)[[362]](#cite_note-364) Between 10,000 and 80,000 people from the [Kingdom of Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah") were similarly [exiled by Babylonia](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity"),[[359]](#cite_note-Smith-Christopher-361) but these people were then returned to [Judea](/wiki/Judea "Judea") by [Cyrus the Great](/wiki/Cyrus_the_Great "Cyrus the Great") of the Persian [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire").[[363]](#cite_note-365)

Many Jews were exiled again by the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").[[364]](#cite_note-366) The 2,000 year dispersion of the [Jewish diaspora](/wiki/Jewish_diaspora "Jewish diaspora") beginning under the Roman Empire,[[365]](#cite_note-367) as Jews were spread throughout the Roman world and, driven from land to land,[[366]](#cite_note-368) settled wherever they could live freely enough to practice their religion. Over the course of the diaspora the center of Jewish life moved from [Babylonia](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Iraq "History of the Jews in Iraq")[[367]](#cite_note-369) to the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Golden_age_of_Jewish_culture_in_the_Iberian_Peninsula "Golden age of Jewish culture in the Iberian Peninsula")[[368]](#cite_note-370) to [Poland](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland "History of the Jews in Poland")[[369]](#cite_note-371) to the [United States](/wiki/Jewish_American "Jewish American")[[370]](#cite_note-372) and, as a result of [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism"), back to [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel").[[356]](#cite_note-Gartner431-358)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Vertreibung_der_Juden_1614.jpg/250px-Vertreibung_der_Juden_1614.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vertreibung_der_Juden_1614.jpg)

Etching of the [expulsion of the Jews from Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurter_Judengasse#The_Fettmilch_Uprising "Frankfurter Judengasse") in [1614](/wiki/Timeline_of_antisemitism "Timeline of antisemitism"). The text says: "1380 persons old and young were counted at the exit of the gate".

There were also many expulsions of Jews during the Middle Ages and Enlightenment in Europe, including: 1290, 16,000 Jews were expelled from England, (see the [Statute of Jewry](/wiki/Statute_of_Jewry "Statute of Jewry")); in 1396, 100,000 from France; in 1421, thousands were expelled from Austria. Many of these Jews settled in [East-Central Europe](/wiki/East-Central_Europe "East-Central Europe"), especially Poland.[[371]](#cite_note-Gartner_11-12-373) Following the [Spanish Inquisition](/wiki/Spanish_Inquisition "Spanish Inquisition") in 1492, the Spanish population of around 200,000 [Sephardic](/wiki/Sephardi "Sephardi") Jews were expelled by the Spanish crown and [Catholic church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church"), followed by expulsions in 1493 in Sicily (37,000 Jews) and Portugal in 1496. The expelled Jews fled mainly to the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), the Netherlands, and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), others migrating to [Southern Europe](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe") and the Middle East.[[372]](#cite_note-Johnson229-231-374)

During the 19th century, France's policies of equal citizenship regardless of religion led to the immigration of Jews (especially from Eastern and Central Europe).[[373]](#cite_note-Johnson_1987,_p._306-375) This contributed to the arrival of millions of Jews in the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"). Over two million Eastern European Jews arrived in the United States from 1880 to 1925.[[374]](#cite_note-376)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Jewish_refugees_Liverpool_1882.jpg/250px-Jewish_refugees_Liverpool_1882.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jewish_refugees_Liverpool_1882.jpg)

Jews fleeing pogroms, 1882

In the latest phase of migrations, the [Islamic Revolution of Iran](/wiki/Iranian_Revolution "Iranian Revolution") caused many [Iranian Jews](/wiki/Iranian_Jews "Iranian Jews") to flee Iran. Most found refuge in the US (particularly [Los Angeles, California](/wiki/Los_Angeles,_California "Los Angeles, California"), and [Long Island, New York](/wiki/Long_Island,_New_York "Long Island, New York")) and Israel. Smaller communities of Persian Jews exist in Canada and Western Europe.[[375]](#cite_note-377) Similarly, when the [Soviet Union collapsed](/wiki/History_of_the_Soviet_Union_(1985%E2%80%931991)#Dissolution_of_the_USSR "History of the Soviet Union (1985–1991)"), many of the Jews in the affected territory (who had been [refuseniks](/wiki/Refusenik "Refusenik")) were suddenly allowed to leave. This produced a wave of migration to Israel in the early 1990s.[[300]](#cite_note-Dosick_2007,_p._340-302)

#### Growth

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Western_Wall%2C_Jerusalem%2C_%2816037897867%29.jpg/250px-Western_Wall%2C_Jerusalem%2C_%2816037897867%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Western_Wall,_Jerusalem,_(16037897867).jpg)

Praying at the [Western Wall](/wiki/Western_Wall "Western Wall")

Israel is the only country with a Jewish population that is consistently growing through [natural population growth](/wiki/Natural_population_growth "Natural population growth"), although the Jewish populations of other countries, in Europe and North America, have recently increased through immigration. In the Diaspora, in almost every country the Jewish population in general is either declining or steady, but [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") and [Haredi](/wiki/Haredi "Haredi") Jewish communities, whose members often shun [birth control](/wiki/Birth_control "Birth control") for religious reasons, have experienced rapid population growth.[[376]](#cite_note-378)

Orthodox and [Conservative Judaism](/wiki/Conservative_Judaism "Conservative Judaism") discourage [proselytism](/wiki/Proselytism "Proselytism") to non-Jews, but many Jewish groups have tried to reach out to the assimilated Jewish communities of the Diaspora in order for them to reconnect to their Jewish roots. Additionally, while in principle [Reform Judaism](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") favours seeking new members for the faith, this position has not translated into active proselytism, instead taking the form of an effort to reach out to non-Jewish spouses of intermarried couples.[[377]](#cite_note-379)

There is also a trend of Orthodox movements reaching out to secular Jews in order to give them a stronger [Jewish identity](/wiki/Jewish_identity "Jewish identity") so there is less chance of intermarriage. As a result of the efforts by these and other Jewish groups over the past 25 years, there has been a trend (known as the [Baal teshuva movement](/wiki/Baal_teshuva_movement "Baal teshuva movement")) for secular Jews to become more religiously observant, though the demographic implications of the trend are unknown.[[378]](#cite_note-380) Additionally, there is also a growing rate of conversion to [Jews by Choice](/wiki/Jews_by_Choice "Jews by Choice") of [gentiles](/wiki/Gentiles "Gentiles") who make the decision to head in the direction of becoming Jews.[[379]](#cite_note-381)

## Contributions

See also: [Lists of Jews](/wiki/Lists_of_Jews "Lists of Jews")

Jewish individuals have played a significant role in the development and growth of [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"),[[60]](#cite_note-Cambridge_University_Historical_Series-62)[[61]](#cite_note-britannica.com-63) advancing many fields of thought, [science and technology](/wiki/Jewish_culture#Science_and_technology "Jewish culture"),[[52]](#cite_note-Daly2013-54) both historically and in modern times,[[380]](#cite_note-382) including through discrete trends in [Jewish philosophy](/wiki/Jewish_philosophy "Jewish philosophy"), [Jewish ethics](/wiki/Jewish_ethics "Jewish ethics")[[54]](#cite_note-Sekine-2005-56) and [Jewish literature](/wiki/Jewish_literature "Jewish literature"),[[52]](#cite_note-Daly2013-54) as well as specific trends in [Jewish culture](/wiki/Jewish_culture "Jewish culture"), including in [Jewish art](/wiki/Jewish_art "Jewish art"), [Jewish music](/wiki/Jewish_music "Jewish music"), [Jewish humor](/wiki/Jewish_humor "Jewish humor"), [Jewish theatre](/wiki/Jewish_theatre "Jewish theatre"), [Jewish cuisine](/wiki/Jewish_cuisine "Jewish cuisine") and [Jewish medicine](/wiki/Jewish_medicine "Jewish medicine").[[56]](#cite_note-Rabin-58)[[57]](#cite_note-Shatzmiller,_Joseph_1995-59) Jews have established various [Jewish political movements](/wiki/Jewish_political_movements "Jewish political movements"),[[52]](#cite_note-Daly2013-54) [religious movements](/wiki/Jewish_religious_movements "Jewish religious movements"), and, through the [authorship of the Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Authorship_of_the_Bible "Authorship of the Bible") and parts of the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"),[[381]](#cite_note-Dimont2004-383)[[382]](#cite_note-Galambush2011-384) provided the foundation for [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam").[[58]](#cite_note-BarclaySweet1996-60)[[59]](#cite_note-Paterson2009-61) More than 20 percent[[383]](#cite_note-385)[[384]](#cite_note-386)[[385]](#cite_note-387)[[386]](#cite_note-388)[[387]](#cite_note-389)[[388]](#cite_note-390) of the awarded [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") have gone to individuals of [Jewish descent](/wiki/List_of_Jewish_Nobel_laureates "List of Jewish Nobel laureates").[[389]](#cite_note-391) Philanthropic giving is a widespread core function among Jewish organizations.[[390]](#cite_note-392)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** The global core Jewish population was estimated at approximately 15,263,500 in 2022. Including individuals who identify as partly Jewish and anyone with one or two Jewish parents increases the estimate to 20,028,800. Adding individuals with a Jewish background but without Jewish parents, and non-Jewish household members living with Jews, yields an enlarged estimate of 22,720,400.[[1]](#cite_note-1)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-53)** The exact world Jewish population, however, is difficult to measure. In addition to issues with census methodology, disputes among proponents of *[halakhic](/wiki/Halakhic "Halakhic")*, secular, political, and ancestral identification factors regarding [who is a Jew](/wiki/Who_is_a_Jew "Who is a Jew") may affect the figure considerably depending on the source.[[51]](#cite_note-Pfeffer-52)

Diaspora: Generation and the Ground of Jewish Diaspora. p. 714](http://nes.berkeley.edu/Web_Boyarin/BoyarinArticles/69%20Diaspora%20(1993).pdf) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20201011234750/https://nes.berkeley.edu/Web_Boyarin/BoyarinArticles/69%20Diaspora%20(1993).pdf) 11 October 2020 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") "...it is crucial to recognize that the Jewish conception of the Land of Israel is similar to the discourse of the Land of many (if not nearly all) "indigenous" peoples of the world. It is profoundly disturbing to hear Jewish attachment to the Land decried as regressive in the same discursive situations in which the attachment of native Americans or Australians to their particular rocks, trees, and deserts is celebrated as an organic connection to the Earth that "we" have lost" p. 714.
